chemicals.concentration_notes: While lectins are present in all parts of the wisteria plant, they have the highest toxic concentration in the seeds and seed pods, making those parts by far the most dangerous
chemicals.description: in cats: Lectins act as severe gastrointestinal irritants. When ingested by a cat, they trigger intense stomach pain, nausea, profuse vomiting (which may contain blood), and diarrhea2.... This massive gastrointestinal distress can subsequently lead to severe dehydration, central nervous system damage, and collapse
chemicals.name: Lectins (specifically noted as irritant lectins)

chemicals.concentration_notes: Like lectins, wisterin is found throughout the entire plant—including the leaves, bark, and flowers—but it is most potently concentrated in the plant's seeds and pods1.... Ingesting the seeds and pods can be fatal to small animals like cats
chemicals.description: in cats: Working alongside the plant's lectins, wisterin is a poisonous compound that contributes to severe systemic illness. It causes drastic gastrointestinal upset, including abdominal pain, bloody vomiting, and diarrhea14. If a cat ingests a large amount, the toxicity can escalate to fatal dehydration, central nervous system damage, and physical collapse
chemicals.name: Wisterin (identified as a toxic glycoside)

description: Wisteria (Wisteria spp.) is a climbing ornamental vine toxic to cats throughout all its parts, with seeds and seed pods carrying the highest toxin concentration. It contains wisterin (a glycoside) and lectin-type proteins that cause significant gastrointestinal distress.
name: Wisteria
safetyNotes: Toxic compounds: wisterin (glycoside) and lectin-type proteins; seeds and seed pods most concentrated; all parts toxic
Mechanism: wisterin irritates the GI mucosa; lectins may inhibit protein synthesis at higher doses
Symptoms: repeated and severe vomiting, diarrhea, drooling, abdominal pain, lethargy; seed ingestion may cause more pronounced effects
Even 2–3 seeds can cause significant symptoms in a cat
Contact your vet if your cat ingests any part of the wisteria plant, especially seeds

symptoms.name: Drooling and nausea [770, 849, Conversation History
symptoms.notes: These are often the initial, outward signs of gastrointestinal irritation caused by the plant's toxic lectins and glycosides [770, 849, Conversation History]. 2. Vomiting (potentially bloody)
symptoms.onset: Typically appears within a few hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Severe vomiting, which may contain blood
symptoms.notes: This profuse vomiting is a direct reaction to the highly irritating compounds (lectins and wisterin) and rapidly leads to severe dehydration [395, 849, Conversation History]. 3. Diarrhea
symptoms.onset: Typically appears within a few hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Diarrhea
symptoms.notes: Combined with severe vomiting, profuse diarrhea causes massive gastrointestinal distress and life-threatening fluid loss [395, 849, Conversation History]. 4. Abdominal Pain
symptoms.onset: Typically appears within a few hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Abdominal pain and stomach cramps [770, 800, 849, Conversation History
symptoms.notes: Arises from the intense irritation of the mucosal lining of the digestive tract [395, 800, Conversation History]. 5. Lethargy, Central Nervous System Damage, and Collapse
symptoms.onset: Typically appears within a few hours of ingestion

symptoms.name: Lethargy, central nervous system (CNS) damage, and physical collapse [395, 770, 849, Conversation History
symptoms.notes: These systemic and neurological symptoms manifest primarily when a cat ingests larger amounts of the plant, particularly the highly concentrated and deadly seeds and seed pods [395, 770, 849, Conversation History]. If left untreated, the severe dehydration and toxicity can be fatal to cats [395, 800
symptoms.onset: Usually progresses following the initial severe gastrointestinal distress that begins within a few h

treatments.description: Gently remove any remaining plant material (especially the highly toxic seeds or pods) from the cat's mouth. Rinse the mouth and offer small sips of water
treatments.name: Oral Decontamination and Plant Removal
treatments.notes: It is highly advised not to induce vomiting at home unless specifically directed to do so by a veterinary professional. Do not give the cat any food or medications without veterinary instruction. If possible, bring a sample of the wisteria plant to the clinic to help the vet confirm the poisoning1.

treatments.description: Once at the clinic, the veterinarian may administer medication to safely induce vomiting, emptying the stomach of the toxic plant material
treatments.name: Medically Induced Emesis (Vomiting)
treatments.notes: This intervention is critical for preventing the absorption of the dangerous lectins and wisterin glycosides. It is most effective when performed as soon as possible after the cat has ingested the plant2. 3. Fluid Therapy

treatments.description: The veterinary team administers fluids to help flush the wisteria toxins out of the cat's body
treatments.name: Intravenous (IV) Fluid Therapy
treatments.notes: Because wisteria poisoning triggers severe, potentially bloody vomiting and profuse diarrhea, cats are at a high risk of life-threatening fluid loss [800, Conversation History]. Aggressive fluid therapy is essential to combat this severe dehydration and stabilize the animal [Conversation History]. 4

treatments.description: The veterinarian will continuously monitor the cat and provide specific medications to manage the intense gastrointestinal and systemic symptoms [800, Conversation History].
treatments.name: Symptomatic Medical Management and Monitoring
treatments.notes: Because wisteria ingestion can escalate to severe stomach cramps, central nervous system (CNS) damage, and physical collapse, the cat may require targeted pharmacological support to ease abdominal pain, stop nausea, and manage any neurological distress until the toxins clear its system [800, Convers
